video_title: Hawks Versus Doves Debate At The Fed
transcript: [music] >> Hello everybody, Ed Your Danny here at uh back home uh June 15th. It's a Monday, 11:00 a.m. New York time. Uh that is not Max, that is Bailey. Bailey's made a guest appearance and as you can see, she's very excited to be here. She's not often uh joining us, but she is here today. Uh I will warn you, she snores, but I've got a Yeti microphone uh which I think uh blocks out background noise, so uh if it gets uh to be uh a little bit uh noisy in the back there, let let let me know and uh I'll ask Bailey to to leave, but she looks pretty comfortable. Uh anyways, we had a very comfortable trip in uh uh Crete and uh Santorini, my wife and I over the past week uh got back Sat- Saturday night. Uh I was a little bit nervous about uh whether we would be able to get back. Not too nervous, uh but I was somewhat concerned that uh there wouldn't be enough jet fuel to get us back and never was an issue. Um so far uh now with the war apparently coming to an end, uh looks like uh there's there's plenty of jet fuel to bring back uh tourists uh to whatever destination they want to uh come back to, but we had we had no problems uh flying there or flying back. Uh the airports were uh were packed. Uh Athens was packed, JFK was packed. Uh we did uh talk to the locals in Crete and Santorini and just kind of asked them uh uh the shop owners and uh uh and taxi drivers about uh tourism and they said that tourism is down uh in Crete and Santorini and they blamed it on the war that it got a lot of people kind of nervous about traveling. Maybe uh uh not not as many people were as adventurous as we were uh willing to take a chance that we'd run out of jet fuel and couldn't come back. Of course, it's not the worst thing that could happen if you go to Greece and you uh can't get back home for a while anyway. So, I had my laptop and I uh did did some work. Uh Toby and Elias uh chipped in uh more than usual on the uh uh morning uh on the on the quick takes. So, that uh that helped, but uh I obviously read these things and edit them and uh kind of uh put put them in in our style. They're still uh kind of learn learning the ropes, but they're learning them very very quickly, I I must say. Um Elias joined us about at the same time as Toby did. Elias is uh stationed and uh lives in Amsterdam and Toby lives in uh Melbourne. And then a few uh uh maybe a year ago William Pesek joined us. Uh and he's in Tokyo. So, uh uh we we uh I guess we're a global firm based on the location of where people are that uh are working with us at our team. And uh basically we're open 24 by 7. So, uh it's fun, it's interesting, and uh we've uh certainly broadened uh our research capabilities uh in terms of what we do to look at the global economy, global financial markets, forward earnings on a global basis, and geopolitics, and and so on. Well, speaking of uh so on, the uh the bull market uh carries on. Um and uh once again uh we've a stress test of the of the economy of the bull market and the stress test has been passed so far remarkably well. Remember the list that we started out the the decade with a pandemic, the lockdowns, the lockdowns uh led to supply chain disruptions because of the pandemic, supply chain disruptions led to inflation, Russian invaded Ukraine, that led to more inflation, central banks realized that they had to raise interest rates, which they did. Uh there was a lot of fear that that would cause a recession, particularly in 2022 we had a bear market. It didn't last that long, it's pretty conventional, 9 months, 25% drop, but there was no recession. Back then I was talking about the most widely anticipated recession of all times, it just wasn't happening. And we were early on on the resilience of the economy, we were early on on the resilience of consumers spending. Uh but that's partly because we staked out the roaring 2020s um narrative back in August of 2020. Once we sort of said, you know, this could be a pretty good uh a pretty good decade for the economy and the stock market, it kind of kept making us look for signs that that was in fact paying out. So, we we took the optimistic side, very very well aware of where what could go wrong in the pessimistic side as well. I I have to once again thank the permabears for doing all this great research on what could go wrong. It helps saves me a lot of time so I can focus on what could go wrong with their forecast and what could go wrong. Uh well, anyways, so what else did we get? Oh, we had the Remember that little mini banking crisis we had in the 2023, then we had the Trump's tariffs last year. Uh we have the uh the war this year, and uh real GDP is at all-time record high in the US. Stock market is almost back to its all-time uh record high. Uh the war presented a uh pretty good uh buying opportunity that did didn't really last uh very long. Um and uh I thought there might be a uh a June swoon swoon, if nothing else it kind of rhymes. Uh but the I the thought was that uh you know, especially after uh what uh we saw on June 5th, remember on June 5th the payroll employment numbers came out, they were very strong for the third month in a row. And then uh the market started to uh price in rate rate hikes instead of uh rate cuts. However, the rate hikes uh are uh the probabilities are scheduled for later this year and uh or early next year. And uh we thought that um there's a the reasonable likelihood given uh the strength of the economy, given that uh the oil shock is having some inflationary consequences, at least uh on on the headline numbers, uh and at the same time, remember that uh let's keep in mind that um uh the point we made about Trump's tariffs, uh people uh the the uh some said that the tariffs had no uh wouldn't have and had no impact on inflation, and uh uh we disagreed with that. We said, "Well, wait a second. Uh you you have to look at what might have happened if the tariffs uh thing never happened. Uh and uh I think we were on course to get down to 2% on the uh uh headline and core CPI and PCEED. Uh we could have done that last year, but the tariffs there's no there's no doubt about it. When you look at the charts, there's no doubt that durable goods inflation definitely picked up and that had to be tariff related. And durable goods inflation continued to be a problem right until a couple of months ago. And now in the latest May data, there's some indications that that's maybe played itself out and it's not having the deflationary consequences that it had for the past year or so. Um but uh so the tariffs what they did is they didn't boost inflation, they just kept it from going lower. I I I hope you get that nuance. It's it's pretty straightforward, I think. So we would have been at 2% by now if it hadn't been for the the tariffs. And then of course we got the war and another oil spike. But this oil spike is reminiscent of what we had in 2022 when Russia invaded Ukraine. But there are some significant differences. The supply chain disruptions aren't as significant now as what we had back in '21 '22 when the pandemic definitely affected global supply chains all around globally all around the world. Um and so this time around the shutting off of the Strait of Hormuz has also had some disruptive impacts on supply chains. A lot of fretting about the availability of helium. I don't think it was people worrying about whether they could get helium balloons for their kids' birthday parties. It was concerned that helium is used for manufacturing semiconductors. Turns out that you talk to some one uh companies that manufacture uh gas and provide gas, put it in in in in containers and all that. Uh there there never really was a serious problem along those uh lines. Uh then there's the fertilizer issue, and that is uh maybe going to have repercussions on on food, though. Uh it's interesting that uh agricultural prices, particularly grain prices, are falling with this news that the war in the Middle East may in fact uh be be be over. Um but um all in all um not only has the economy been resilient, the consumer has been resilient, uh but as a result of that, earnings have been uh fabulous. Uh fabulous earnings momentum is what I call it, and that's SHEMO, not to be confused with with FOMO. Uh let's look at uh some of the latest um SHEMO numbers here, uh which is what's driving the market. Also, I was saying uh and so I I thought there might be a June swoon. I was kind of hoping there'd be a buying opportunity, but seems this market doesn't want to give us buying opportunities anymore. Uh speaking of buying opportunities, like all my broker called me up uh a couple of times over the past uh few weeks and asked me if I had any interest in uh participating in this uh SpaceX IPO. I said, "Yeah, I think so. Uh maybe maybe like a thousand shares." Um I figured, you know, it's kind of a with Goldman and Morgan Stanley would be well-managed, and uh there'd probably be a kind of a a a good gain in it. Um well, I went on vacation. I really didn't want to be uh focusing on the market, and besides uh when I invest my own money, I tend to invest in ETFs. I I don't want to really kind of get distracted from my job by by uh looking at individual stocks, and SpaceX is such a controversial, important uh uh maybe uh life-changing uh company that I wanted to be able to maintain my objectivity. My objectivity right now tells me that uh you know, get get get me 5 minutes with Musk and I'll tell him there's really no reason to go to Mars. There's absolutely nothing there. Uh there's no reason to put uh data centers in outer space. They'll probably get uh hit by space junk. Uh put them uh in the ocean floor. I saw on LinkedIn an article saying that the Chinese were putting these things in in in the ocean floor. And by the way, not related to uh to uh SpaceX, uh I would advise uh Tesla, uh his company, to uh manufacture hybrids. Anyways, I got got that off my chest. I I feel much better now. Uh but uh the the SpaceX went went well, very well managed. Uh obviously, uh then uh the uh any concerns uh about the Fed uh have been in uh somewhat uh reduced by the by the uh better than expected uh core inflation numbers we got for the PPI and and the CPI. Uh and just uh kind of a sense that uh the uh when the FOMC uh announces uh sends out its statement on uh Wednesday, the perception is that uh it uh it may not uh be biased towards easing, uh but it might be biased into a neutral stance. Uh we're still kind of holding on to the idea that it might actually be biased towards a tightening stance. Uh And that's because uh you know, the the Fed hasn't uh gotten its target right. Uh its target is 2%. They have They've been above that for 5 years now. And so uh Um from that perspective, uh the Fed hasn't been restrictive enough. Uh This rebound in inflation, at least in a short-term basis, lowers the real Fed funds rate. Uh more importantly, Elias I uh I've been thinking that uh with uh all the AI capital spending boosting the economy with the consumers uh particularly retiring baby boomers relying on their 89 trillion dollars of uh of of of of retirement assets that uh real GDP growth uh is uh more solid and therefore the real Fed funds rate um the normal or neutral Fed funds rate at which all this can happen is actually higher than otherwise would be the case. If that's so, then um the Fed funds rate, the actual Fed funds rate, isn't really even slightly restrictive. It might be actually a bit too easy and I think that's kind of confirmed by the fact of the way the stock market's been uh roaring to to the upside. But again, let's uh go look at some charts here. Uh so we'll [clears throat] share and we'll go to let's start out with the Fed. So uh we Elias and I uh went through the hawks versus doves debate at at the Fed. Um and um admittedly, there's a lot that the doves can uh point to and of of course one of the key doves is uh uh Kevin uh in fact the new Fed chair Kevin Warsh. And uh here we have uh the oil price spike back in 2022. Uh inflation was already going up back then and supply chain disruptions. This time around, inflation was moderate and uh uh looked like it was continuing to head in the right direction towards 2% over the next couple of years. Uh that's what was in the uh summary of economic projections by the uh by the Fed. This time around uh we didn't get up to 150 to 200, which some people were were fretting about. Uh even a couple of weeks ago I uh I I I I kind of uh flinched uh after listening to uh what uh the head of uh some top officials at Exxon and at Chevron were saying, and they were saying that uh you know, if this war doesn't end soon, the price is going to go up to $150 cuz uh inventories were uh getting uh right down at the bottom of the literally the right down at the bottom of the barrel. Uh well, looks like the war's over and uh looks like there were some other factors that at play here. Looks like the Chinese weren't uh needing as much oil as uh as pe- people thought. The economy is weak. They also have a lot of uh strategic uh reserves piled up. Uh and all in all uh it was really quite amazing given the news that was going on back here that uh we only spiked up twice at to about 118. And now as of uh uh Friday, we're down to 87, and obviously even lower than that today. Uh so, with that uh reduces the uh inflation concerns uh that uh would uh we'd all have if this thing just uh re- remained uh around 100. Uh we're already starting to see that uh gasoline futures prices are coming down. That means that the national retail price is going to come down, and then the CPI gasoline should should come down. Uh again, uh one big difference between now and '21 and '22 is uh wage price spiral. Back here, the labor market was very tight. Wage inflation using average hourly earnings or using the employment cost index. We saw inflation going up a lot. Then inflation came down. Wage inflation came down quite a bit. So, we're not we're not looking like like what we had back here with the wage price spiral. By the way, this is a chart of uh demand versus supply in the labor market and we remain in that fairly tight equilibrium. And if you take labor demand minus labor supply, you get this series. Okay? And then we on that put the wage growth tracker, which we actually like better than the average hourly earnings. Average hourly earnings is sensitive to the composition of labor um as as we saw back here when a lot of uh low-wage workers lost their jobs, uh average hourly earnings spiked up because there were a lot more people uh uh who were uh in the high high-wage area of of the labor market and so that what didn't adjust for the composition, the uh wage growth tracker does account for the composition and you can see that uh demand and supply are kind of in equilibrium and maybe demand is starting to grow faster than supply and maybe that'll put some upward pressure on wages, but for the here and now that's not the case. Uh Another concern that the Fed uh uh has is not only wage price spirals kind of uh taking a uh supply shock like an oil price spike uh and worrying that it'll spread to the uh other prices in the economy is not only a wage price spiral, but also whether inflation expectations are well-anchored. And here's the one year ahead from the FRB New York consumer survey and you can see it shot up pretty dramatically here. Even the three year went up. But so far so good. This is pretty pretty calm. So that's that's good news. Unit labor costs we've been actually a lot about that that this time around is even less. Look at here unit labor costs back here did shoot up as wages rose quite sharply and productivity did not offset that very much. Now we've got productivity offsetting wages and you can see that unit labor costs are down to 0.5% on a on a year-over-year basis. So the wage growth tracker again this is the wage growth tracker job stairs versus job switchers and you can see that there's a much incentive to switch jobs these days. You don't really get much of a pay pay boost. So what's the reason for even thinking that they might go for a tightening bias? Well, there's the quaint thing about the mandate. The dual mandate says that they want to keep the unemployment rate down and inflation down and now the risks clearly are more on the inflation side than they are on the unemployment rate side. So uh if the risks were sort of in balance a neutral might be the right stance but uh given that we still have some inflationary pressures working through the system maybe that's not the case. And again uh the uh Fed officials most of them recall them that they made a mistake in 2021 2022 and that is maybe not moving fast enough in 2022 to raise interest rates. And so they had this idea that inflation was transitory, they started to recognize it might be persistent, and then they acted quickly to raise rates, and then inflation came down. They may have those same kind of concerns. I mean, I don't really think the Fed is as almighty as they they think it is, and I think a lot of these things happen. Inflation is just not, in my mind, not just a monetary phenomenon. But, as long as they think so, we have to kind of think the way they do. And they may be thinking that if they don't indicate that they're willing to tighten in the face of a tight labor market with some inflationary pressures, that that will affect um inflation's inflationary psychology. Meanwhile, as I said, Elias and I uh thought about it, and with inflation higher than it had been, then the real rate um uh the the real federal funds rate has actually gone down, while meanwhile, uh the uh the the neutral federal funds rate at which we get nirvana in the economy, that's going up because of uh AI capital spending. And by the way, AI capital spending, we we agree that it's a productivity enhancer, and that it's going to lower inflation, but for the year and a half, AI capital spending is actually quite inflationary. It's boosted commodity prices, it's boosted uh electricity prices. And uh when you look at the CPI and PPI on the kind of hardware side, on computers and semiconductors, but we all know those those prices have gone up. Um and uh meanwhile, the 10-year and the 2-year, well, the 2-year here, but the 10-year is the same thing. The 10-year is uh saying [clears throat] that um the Fed should be raising interest rates. And if they don't, then if they indicate that uh they're buying into Warsh's idea that no need to worry about inflation, productivity is going to make a difference, the bond market may uh uh may not buy yields may go up on the on the fears that the Fed isn't being vigilant enough. Uh labor market is tightening from the supply side. This is what's going on with the growth rate of labor force of people who are foreign-born. Obviously, that has to do with the administration's immigration policies. Uh people like me, I'm still working for a living. Uh I don't play golf, so I don't know what I do myself, but I but I didn't do this. And besides, it's a lot of fun. Um but as you can see, the participation rate of 55 years and older, that's been been coming down. Uh especially of men, and that's reflects retirement. Uh so, the supply of labor is is tightening, the demand seems to be picking up, and that increases the chances that maybe wages will start start start moving up. I think the other compelling argument the most compelling argument is that uh the inflation rate has been above 2% for 5 years. The Fed hasn't been particularly successful at all in getting to their target. Um maybe monetary policy is not not as easy to doesn't have as much of an impact on the economy and inflation as Fed officials like to believe, but if that's the way they believe, we got to go with with what they're doing. But supercore is something that uh we we can thank Fed Chair Jerome Powell for coming up with. And supercore inflation just seems to be stuck here around 3 and 1/2%. And that's well above what we had back before before the pandemic. Couple of more things here. Let's look at FEMA with the the earning story. I think that's it. Yeah, this is the US market call. We now have a US global call again with uh Elias and Toby and uh the rest of our team uh we're we're we're pretty well staffed now and able to cover uh even more than we had in the past. Hope you're you're all see that. Um but uh speak speaking of SpaceX 75 billion is what was raised uh in a market where just the Wilshire 5000, not the entire market is 74 trillion dollars. So, they raised 70 75 billion. Uh of course, that number um is kind of the uh the entry price the the entry uh funds raised by SpaceX because uh at some point here uh there'll be a lot more stock that uh will will be sold in the market place. Uh but still, this explains why things Let me just move this over here. Uh why things went so well. Um so, this we've looked at before. We were actually in a bear market in crude oil until the war started. And uh sure sure feels as though we could be uh surprised to the downside. I'm already surprised to the downside uh given all the talk about all the infrastructure that's uh has been uh weakened and that uh there's going to be a lot of demand to increase strategic petroleum reserves again. Um but on the earning side um you know, uh speaking of SpaceX again uh it kind of sh shut shot right back up here and here we have I'm sorry. Let me I should have turned that off. Okay. This is uh this is a very smooth production as you can see. Uh anyways, uh this is uh, long-term earnings growth. And you can see long-term earnings growth has kind of shot up here uh, to uh, 24%. That's annual. That's an annual rate. And it's uh, obviously loony tune. Uh, so maybe if you want to be concerned about uh, FEMO, uh, fabulous earnings momentum, maybe it's too fabulous. Maybe our analysts are getting a little bit too uh, heady about uh, what can be accomplished. Uh, I mean the likelihood that we'll have 24% earnings growth in the S&P 500 every year for the next three to five years is about as as as as likely as we're going to have uh, uh, get get get to Mars uh, uh, by over that period. Uh, and by [clears throat] the way, Elon, there's nothing there. Okay, forward earnings are something that's uh, maybe more realistic. Uh, as we can see that forward earnings are up to $366 a share, all-time record high. The analyst expectations for 2027 just keeps marching higher and higher. 2026 higher and higher. That's FEMO. More FEMO here. FEMO S&P 500 forward earnings. Uh, the 400 and the and the 600 all at the record highs. Um, maybe there maybe I can uh, declare some victory on the June swoon. I'm going to take credit for June swoon on one day there June and uh, June uh, uh, the June uh, uh, 15th when the market was down over 2 and 1/2%. That's stronger than expected payroll employment which uh, got everybody kind of thinking more along our lines that there's more of a problem with with with with inflation. Um, sorry that there's um, um, yeah, that there was that the Fed might actually uh, be going on a tightening course. Uh, well, uh, there's another soon here. This is the Magnificent Seven mags divided by the impressive 493. And I kind of view this June soon as more of a rotation than a correction. And I think it may still be going on here. You can see this in the growth rates of the ETFs year to date X mags up 12.6 S&P 500 8.6 and then minus 1.6. I guess that's kind of covers probably more than you wanted me to cover. But hopefully I I covered what what you're interested in. So let me take a couple of questions here. David, does LTEG include unrealized gains of securities that companies own? No, it doesn't. LTEG is totally a survey notion. I think it's Thompson Reuters basically gets from analysts what they what they think earnings are going to do. I mean clearly some analysts may be factoring in capital gains in stock portfolios that companies own. But that's such a recent development. I kind of doubt that any analyst is putting that into their Excel spreadsheet. So I think it's just you know when analysts look at their companies individually it's they they can see they can see moon shots. And when you put all those moon shots together you get a moon shot that just doesn't make any sense. So there is I say there's somewhere there is some sign of irrational exuberance on the earning side. Usually irrational exuberance describes what's happening on the earning side on the PE side. >> Um, note that the reality of a de de fanged Iran now seems apparent when you combine that with the Abraham Accords and the re-establishment of the US as a predominant military superpower, doesn't that ratchet up the roaring 20s secular narrative a notch going forward? Uh, Chip, I'll take any argument that supports our roaring 2020s scenario. That kind of works for me. Uh, but I don't uh need the US to be a superpower to All I really need is for the economy to continue to grow without a without a recession and the stock market to get to 10,000 by the end of the decade, by the end of 2029. And remember, if you just use your fingers to count, we are six we are 7 years into the roaring 2020s. By the end of the year, we're just going to have to get three more years right. And the way things are going, I'm starting to wonder whether uh uh 10,000 might occur between before 2029. I'll take one more question here uh from Kevin. Uh, before the peace agreement, gold took a hit. Uh, either central banks reduced buying or sold gold to support things like a country currency or pay for the higher oil prices. I know you said if gold drops below 4K, you will reconsider your stand. Any Yeah, actually uh no, we we said that uh 4K was the magic number and uh uh couple days ago, we said that it I think we got down to like 4100. We said that that's close enough and that might very well have been a uh important support. So, if you look at uh let's go back here to the uh Okay, so here's the This, by the way, is on the website, the quick takes. If you go here to uh the peace dividend one on the global side, this is what we're We we showed this actually I think on Yeah, on Sunday. And but I I was looking at this chart a couple days ago and I said, "Okay, we've broken through the 200-day so there's more downside. Next stop is 4,000 and if it holds that, that's probably a buying opportunity and so far so good." So I I wasn't saying that if we drop below 4,000 I was I was saying if we drop below 4,000 then it's it's going still lower, but I was counting on uh this channel to hold. Uh this channel isn't extremely scientific. I took a ruler and drew it here. At least I did that with the computer and um it's uh working pretty well so far. All right. Have a great week. Lots of fun. Lots of interesting things going on in our business. I hope you're doing extremely well in the market and we'll we'll be back next week. Thank you. >> [music] [music]
